pirates.name: Christopher Myngs
pirates.ship_type: 46-gun fourth-rate frigate (earlier the Marston Moor)

flag.description: English naval colours; a King's officer leading buccaneer auxiliaries under commission — no pirate flag

notable_crew.name: Sir Thomas Whetstone
notable_crew.role: buccaneer captain under Myngs, 1662

notable_crew.name: Edward Mansfield
notable_crew.role: buccaneer commander in the Campeche raid, 1663

lading: 22 chests of royal silver and plunder worth ~1.5 million pesos from the Cumana-Puerto Cabello-Coro raid, 1659
church bells, guns and ransom goods from the sack of Santiago de Cuba, 1662
pirates.fate: Returned to England 1663; died a vice-admiral of wounds taken in the Four Days' Battle, 1666

track.note: arrives on the Jamaica station with the Marston Moor; Port Royal becomes his base
raids Cumana, Puerto Cabello and Coro; takes 22 chests of royal silver, then is sent home accused of embezzling it
storms Santiago de Cuba with a buccaneer fleet, October 1662
sacks San Francisco de Campeche with ~1,400 men, February 1663; badly wounded
back at Port Royal; recalled to England

pirates.name: François l'Olonnais (Jean-David Nau)
pirates.ship_type: 8-ship buccaneer flotilla, 1666; flagship of ~10 guns

flag.description: no flag recorded; sailed with French commissions from Tortuga — possibly the red banner of no quarter

notable_crew.name: Moses van Vin
notable_crew.role: second in command (per Exquemelin)

notable_crew.name: Pierre le Picard
notable_crew.role: captain under l'Olonnais on the Honduras cruise

lading: ~260,000 pieces of eight, plate, jewels and gems from Maracaibo and Gibraltar, 1666 (Exquemelin)
cacao, indigo and ship's stores from Honduras prizes, 1667-68
pirates.fate: Wrecked near the Darién coast; killed and reportedly eaten by Kuna people, c.1669 (Exquemelin)

track.note: musters 8 ships and ~660 men in the Tortuga roadstead
forces the bar of the Lake and sacks Maracaibo
stands out of the Gulf of Venezuela with ~260,000 pesos after torturing Gibraltar for ransom
the plunder squandered at Tortuga and Port Royal; a new cruise planned
lurks off Puerto Caballos; takes a Spanish ship and sacks the shore
marches inland to San Pedro and is mauled; the cruise sours
grounds his ship on cays off the Mosquito Coast; the company breaks up
killed by the Kuna in the Gulf of Darién — 'torn to pieces alive' (Exquemelin)

pirates.name: Henry Morgan
pirates.ship_type: 22-gun frigate (ex-French Cour Volant); admiral of up to 36 buccaneer vessels

flag.description: English colours under Jamaica privateering commissions from Governor Modyford — a privateer-buccaneer, never under a black flag

notable_crew.name: Edward Collier
notable_crew.role: vice-admiral of the 1670-71 fleet

notable_crew.name: Joseph Bradley
notable_crew.role: captain; took Chagres castle, mortally wounded, 1671

notable_crew.name: John Morris
notable_crew.role: captain and longtime consort

lading: Portobelo ransom and plunder, ~250,000 pesos, 1668
~125,000 pesos and the Magdalena's salvage from Maracaibo, 1669
Panama sack: ~400,000 pesos in plate, coin and slaves, 1671 — and bitter, thin shares
pirates.fate: Arrested for Panama as a treaty embarrassment, knighted instead (1674); died Lieutenant-Governor of Jamaica, 1688

track.note: lands 500 men on the south Cuban coast for the Puerto Príncipe raid, March 1668
storms Portobelo's Iron Castle, July 1668; the town ransomed for 100,000 pesos
fleet rendezvous at Île à Vache, the buccaneers' mustering ground
HMS Oxford explodes under him at the Île à Vache council of war, January 1669; Morgan survives
sacks Maracaibo and Gibraltar; destroys the Armada de Barlovento at the bar with a fireship, April 1669
musters 36 ships and ~1,800 men at Île à Vache under Modyford's commission
Bradley takes San Lorenzo de Chagres; Satisfaction wrecks on Lajas Reef at the river mouth, December 1670
marches up the Chagres and across the isthmus; Panama taken and burned, 28 January 1671
returns to Port Royal; the peace with Spain makes his triumph a scandal

pirates.name: Michel de Grammont ('le Chevalier')
pirates.ship_type: 50-gun ship Le Hardi

flag.description: French filibuster under Petit-Goâve commissions; ensign unrecorded

lading: plunder and prisoner ransoms from the sack of Veracruz, 1683 (co-commanded with de Graaf and Van Hoorn)
salvaged stores and guns from d'Estrées' fleet wrecked at Las Aves, 1678
plunder of Campeche and its hinterland, 1685 — so thin he burned the town in spite
pirates.fate: Named lieutenant du roi of Saint-Domingue 1686; lost at sea with all hands that autumn

track.note: d'Estrées' fleet wrecks on Las Aves; Grammont's filibusters salvage the wrecks and carry on alone
leads ~700 men into the Lake of Maracaibo, holding the region six months
storms La Guaira, the port of Caracas, with a handful of men; badly wounded in the retreat
with de Graaf and Van Hoorn surprises Veracruz, May 1683 — the city held hostage for ransom
sacks Campeche with de Graaf, July-August 1685; burns it when ransom is refused
vanishes with all hands in a storm north of the Bahama Channel, bound to raid the Florida coast (position conjectural)

pirates.name: Laurens de Graaf
pirates.ship_type: ship of ~50 guns (prize); earlier the 26-gun Tigre and the captured payroll frigate Princesa

flag.description: Dutch-born filibuster under French commission; ensign unrecorded

notable_crew.name: Michiel Andrieszoon
notable_crew.role: lieutenant and consort captain

notable_crew.name: Nicolaas van Hoorn
notable_crew.role: co-commander at Veracruz; died of a wound from his duel with de Graaf, 1683

lading: the Santo Domingo garrison payroll, ~120,000 pesos in silver, taken with the Princesa, 1682
Veracruz ransom plunder, 1683
cacao and coin from prizes off Cartagena, Christmas 1683
pirates.fate: Pardoned and commissioned by France; defended Saint-Domingue 1691-95; later aided Iberville's Louisiana voyage; died 1704

track.note: takes the Princesa off Puerto Rico with the Santo Domingo payroll aboard — ~120,000 pesos
leads the surprise of Veracruz with Grammont and Van Hoorn, May 1683
with Andrieszoon and Yankey defeats the squadron sent out from Cartagena, Christmas 1683
fits out at Petit-Goâve under French protection
sacks Campeche with Grammont, July 1685
outfights the Armada de Barlovento's Regla and Concepción in the Yucatán Channel and escapes, September 1685
now a French officer, fights in the defence of Cap-Français against the Anglo-Spanish invasion

pirates.name: Bartholomew Sharp
pirates.ship_type: captured Spanish armada ship Santísima Trinidad, ~400 tons

flag.description: company colours on the 1680 Darién expedition: a red flag with white and green ribbons (Ringrose's journal)

notable_crew.name: Basil Ringrose
notable_crew.role: navigator and journal-keeper

notable_crew.name: William Dampier
notable_crew.role: crewman (quit the cruise April 1681)

notable_crew.name: Lionel Wafer
notable_crew.role: surgeon (left with Dampier across the isthmus)

lading: plate and coin from El Santo Rosario, July 1681 — plus 700 pigs of silver mistaken for tin and left aboard (Ringrose)
the Rosario's derrotero — the Spanish South Sea atlas whose surrender bought Sharp's acquittal
wine, coin and church plate from La Serena and coastal prizes
pirates.fate: Tried for piracy in London 1682 and acquitted — the captured derrotero bought royal favour; died in debt on St Thomas, c.1702

track.note: the buccaneers land at Golden Island and cross the Darién on foot, April 1680
battle of Perico in Panama Bay; the Trinity taken and made flagship, May 1680
sacks La Serena, Chile, December 1680; Sharp commands after Sawkins' death
winters at Juan Fernández; the Miskito man William left behind in the scramble to sea, January 1681
bloodily repulsed at Arica, 30 January 1681; Watling killed, Sharp re-elected
takes El Santo Rosario off Cabo Pasado, 29 July 1681 — silver, and the priceless derrotero
runs south past Cape Horn in November storms — the first English crew to round it west-to-east
landfall off Barbados, January 1682, after 7 months without touching land; the company disperses at Antigua

pirates.name: Edward Davis
pirates.ship_type: 36-gun ship (Danish prize taken off Sierra Leone, 1683)

flag.description: no flag recorded

notable_crew.name: John Cooke
notable_crew.role: original captain; died off Costa Rica, 1684

notable_crew.name: Lionel Wafer
notable_crew.role: surgeon

notable_crew.name: William Dampier
notable_crew.role: crewman (left with Swan's Cygnet, 1686)

lading: shares of the Guayaquil raid's ransom, 1687
Pacific coastal plunder — by 1688 reportedly ~5,000 pesos a man (Wafer)
pirates.fate: Came in under James II's pardon; arrested with Wafer in Virginia 1688, released with most of his loot 1692; thereafter unrecorded

track.note: Cooke's company takes a Danish 36-gun ship off Sierra Leone — renamed Batchelor's Delight
rounds Cape Horn for the South Sea
Cooke dies off Cabo Blanco; Davis elected captain, July 1684
the buccaneer fleet outmanoeuvred and scattered by the Armada del Mar del Sur in Panama Bay, June 1685
joins the sack of León and Realejo on the Nicaragua coast, August 1685
winters and refits at Juan Fernández
cruises off Guayaquil during Picard and Hutt's raid; shares the ransom
sights 'Davis Land' in the empty South Pacific — a legend that later sent ships hunting toward Easter Island
comes in at Point Comfort, Virginia, under the pardon — and is arrested with Wafer

pirates.name: Jan Willems ('Yankey')
pirates.ship_type: barque, later a prize frigate of ~30 guns

flag.description: Dutch filibuster under French commission from Petit-Goâve; ensign unrecorded

lading: share of the Cartagena squadron prizes, Christmas 1683
Veracruz plunder, 1683
pirates.fate: Drops from the record after 1687-88; reported dead on the Mosquito Coast (uncertain)

track.note: fits out at Petit-Goâve among the filibuster captains
sails in the Veracruz expedition under de Graaf and Grammont
with de Graaf and Andrieszoon beats the Cartagena squadron and takes its ships, Christmas 1683
at the sack of Campeche
lingers on the Mosquito Coast as French commissions dry up; last firm reports

pirates.name: Salé Rovers (corsairs of the Bou Regreg)
pirates.ship_type: shallow-draught xebecs and square-rigged 'Sallee men' of 10-24 guns, able to cross the river bar

flag.description: Moroccan corsair colours — crescent devices on red or green; varied by raïs and period

lading: Christian captives held for ransom — the principal 'cargo' of every cruise
wine, cloth, salt and fish from Iberian and Atlantic prizes
pirates.fate: Corsairing declines after the 1721 Anglo-Moroccan treaty ransomed 296 captives; sporadic cruises continue for decades

track.note: habitual cruising ground off Cape St Vincent for Iberia-bound shipping (representative cruise)
lying in the Madeira approaches for wine ships (representative cruise)
in the Gulf of Cádiz among the Indies-trade stragglers (representative cruise)
off the Canaries; European states begin paying tribute or convoying (representative cruise)
off Cape Spartel, within sight of home (representative cruise)
prizes taken in the Lisbon approaches after the Utrecht peace fills the sea with unescorted trade
over the Bou Regreg bar where no frigate can follow; the 1721 British treaty will ransom 296 captives

pirates.name: Thomas Tew
pirates.ship_type: 70-ton Bermuda sloop, 8 guns

flag.description: black flag with an arm holding a cutlass — a 19th-century attribution, not period-documented

lading: gold, silver, ivory, spices and silks worth ~£100,000 from the 1693 Red Sea prize — shares of £1,200-£3,000 a man
pirates.fate: Killed by a cannon shot attacking the Fateh Muhammed at the Red Sea mouth, September 1695 (Johnson)

track.note: rounds the Cape, having talked his crew out of the Gorée privateering commission: 'a gold chain or a wooden leg'
takes a Mughal ship bound from India to Mocha at the Bab-el-Mandeb — almost without loss
waters at Île Sainte-Marie, the new Madagascar pirate mart
home to Newport, April 1694 — the toast of Rhode Island, his backers repaid fourteen-fold
sails again in November with a Fletcher privateering commission bought in New York
joins Henry Every's six-ship flotilla off the Bab-el-Mandeb
killed attacking the Fateh Muhammed, September 1695 — 'a shot carried away the rim of his belly' (Johnson)

pirates.name: Henry Every ('Long Ben')
pirates.ship_type: ex-privateer Charles II, 46 guns, razeed for speed

flag.description: skull in profile with bandana and earring over crossed bones, on black — traditional attribution only

notable_crew.name: Joseph Dawson
notable_crew.role: mate; pleaded guilty at the 1696 trial

notable_crew.name: William May
notable_crew.role: crewman; hanged at Execution Dock, 1696

lading: the Ganj-i-Sawai treasure, September 1695: gold, silver coin and jewels valued £325,000-£600,000 — the richest pirate haul of the century
coin and goods from the Fateh Muhammed, taken days earlier
pirates.fate: Vanished after landing in Ireland, June 1696, despite the first worldwide manhunt; six of his men hanged at Execution Dock

track.note: leads the mutiny aboard the laid-up privateer Charles II in A Coruña road, 7 May 1694; renames her Fancy
plunders three English ships among the Cape Verdes
takes two Danish ships near Príncipe in the Gulf of Guinea
careens at Johanna (Anjouan); razees the Fancy — now one of the fastest ships in any ocean
commands the pirate flotilla at the Bab-el-Mandeb — Tew, Faro, Want, Wake, Mayes — waiting for the Mocha fleet
runs down and storms the Mughal Ganj-i-Sawai off the Surat coast, 7-11 September 1695; atrocities aboard provoke an imperial crisis
waters at Ascension, turtling, on the long run back across the Atlantic
buys Governor Trott's hospitality at New Providence; the Fancy, given as a bribe, is let drift onto the reef
lands quietly in the north of Ireland, June 1696 — and is never reliably seen again

sources: 1696 trial records (Tryals of Joseph Dawson et al.)

pirates.name: William Kidd
pirates.ship_type: purpose-built hybrid oared galley-frigate, 34 guns, 287 tons

flag.description: sailed under a Crown privateering and pirate-hunting commission and English colours; no pirate flag

notable_crew.name: William Moore
notable_crew.role: gunner; killed by Kidd with a bucket, October 1697

notable_crew.name: Robert Bradinham
notable_crew.role: surgeon; turned King's evidence

notable_crew.name: Darby Mullins
notable_crew.role: crewman; hanged with Kidd, 1701

lading: the Quedagh Merchant's cargo, January 1698: silks, muslins, calico, sugar, opium and iron, sold along the coast for ~£10,000 in gold and goods
the Rouparelle (renamed November), taken under French passes, November 1697
pirates.fate: Hanged at Execution Dock, 23 May 1701 — the rope broke and he was hanged again; gibbeted at Tilbury Point

track.note: sails from New York, 6 September 1696, with a pirate-hunting commission and powerful Whig backers
falls in with a Royal Navy squadron off the Cape and slips away by night rather than lose men to the press
waters at Tuléar, southwest Madagascar, January 1697
careens at Mohéli in the Comoros; some fifty men die of fever
lies in wait for the Mocha fleet at the Bab-el-Mandeb, August 1697 — driven off by its East India Company escort: the line into piracy crossed
prowls the Malabar coast; kills gunner William Moore with an ironbound bucket, 30 October 1697; takes the Rouparelle in November
takes the Quedagh Merchant off Cochin under French passes, 30 January 1698 — silks, calico, sugar, opium, iron
at Île Sainte-Marie most of the crew defect to Culliford's Mocha; the leaking Adventure Galley is stripped and scuttled
off Anguilla learns he has been proclaimed a pirate throughout the empire
hides the Quedagh Merchant up the Higuey river by Isla Catalina and buys a sloop to go north and bargain
comes in to Boston trusting Bellomont's word; arrested 6 July 1699, the Gardiners Island gold dug up as evidence

pirates.name: Robert Culliford
pirates.ship_type: ex-East India Company frigate Mocha, ~30-40 guns

flag.description: no flag recorded

lading: share of the Great Mohammed's coin, September 1698 — ~£130,000 taken with Chivers' Soldado
pirates.fate: Took the 1699 pardon at Sainte-Marie; it was ruled invalid and he was condemned 1701, then reprieved to testify against Samuel Burgess; thereafter unrecorded

track.note: with the crew that stole Kidd's privateer Blessed William at Antigua, February 1690 — the start of a long feud (position approximate); years of obscurity and Mughal imprisonment follow
commands the mutinied EIC frigate Mocha (Resolution), cruising the Nicobars and Malacca approaches
at Île Sainte-Marie; Kidd's crew desert to him almost to a man
with Dirk Chivers takes the Great Mohammed in the Red Sea, September 1698 — ~£130,000
surrenders to Commodore Littleton's squadron at Sainte-Marie under the royal pardon

pirates.name: Dirk Chivers
pirates.ship_type: prize ship Soldado, ~28-30 guns

flag.description: no flag recorded

lading: ransoms extorted from shipping held in Calicut road, 1696
share of the Great Mohammed's coin, September 1698
pirates.fate: Surrendered under the royal pardon at Île Sainte-Marie, 1699, and shipped for home; thereafter unrecorded

track.note: reputedly in the strait with Every's flotilla aboard Faro's Portsmouth Adventure (Rogozinski)
holds ships to ransom in Calicut road; writes the famous defiance — 'we acknowledge no country, having sold our own'
the Soldado and Culliford's Resolution take the Great Mohammed in the Red Sea
takes Commodore Littleton's pardon at Sainte-Marie

pirates.name: John Bowen
pirates.ship_type: 50-gun ex-slaver; later the Scots ship Speedy Return and the Defiance

flag.description: no flag recorded

notable_crew.name: Nathaniel North
notable_crew.role: quartermaster, later a captain in his own right (Johnson vol. II)

lading: Red Sea and Malabar plunder aboard the Speaker — coin and goods lost and partly saved in the 1702 wreck
~£70,000 in coin and goods from Mughal ships taken off Surat with Howard's Prosperous, 1703
pirates.fate: Retired rich to Bourbon, March 1704; died of an intestinal illness within six months (Johnson)

track.note: the Speaker cruises the Red Sea mouth for the Mocha and pilgrim trade
takes Indian prizes along the Malabar coast
the Speaker wrecks on the reef at Grand Port, Mauritius, January 1702; the courteous Dutch sell the survivors a sloop
seizes the Scots ship Speedy Return on the Madagascar coast while her captain trades ashore (coast position approximate)
with Thomas Howard's Prosperous takes great Mughal ships off Surat — ~£70,000; crews merge into the Defiance
comes in to the Bourbon roadstead and retires ashore with his fortune, March 1704

pirates.name: Benjamin Hornigold
pirates.ship_type: large Jamaica-built sloop, ~30 guns — for a time the most powerful ship at Nassau

flag.description: no flag recorded; famously claimed to attack only foreign ships, sparing the English

notable_crew.name: Edward Thatch (Blackbeard)
notable_crew.role: protégé, then consort captain 1716-17

notable_crew.name: Samuel Bellamy
notable_crew.role: sailed in his company, 1716

lading: Spanish salvage silver and coin funneled through Nassau after the 1715 plate-fleet wrecks
sugar, rum and dry goods from Florida Strait prizes
pirates.fate: Pardoned July 1718; turned pirate-hunter for Woodes Rogers; wrecked on an uncharted reef and lost, 1719 (Johnson)

track.note: periagua and sloop raids on Spanish trade in the Florida Strait from governorless New Providence
cruises the plate-fleet wreck coast as salvage silver floods into Nassau and the Republic of Pirates blooms
cruises the old Bahama Channel with Bellamy and Thatch among his company
at the height of his standing among the Flying Gang at Nassau
deposed by vote for refusing to plunder English ships; keeps the Ranger
takes the King's pardon from Woodes Rogers, July 1718
as Rogers' pirate-hunter runs down John Augur's relapsed gang near Exuma — eight hang at Nassau in December
lost when his ship strikes an uncharted reef on a trading voyage toward New Spain (position conjectural, per Johnson)

pirates.name: Samuel 'Black Sam' Bellamy
pirates.ship_type: galley-built ex-slaver, 300 tons, 28 guns mounted; earlier the Sultana and the sloop Marianne

flag.description: large black flag with a death's head and crossed bones (survivor testimony at the 1717 Boston trial)

notable_crew.name: Paulsgrave Williams
notable_crew.role: consort captain of the sloop Marianne

notable_crew.name: John Julian
notable_crew.role: Miskito pilot; survived the wreck

notable_crew.name: Thomas Davis
notable_crew.role: forced carpenter; survived, acquitted at Boston

lading: plunder of ~50 prizes aboard the Whydah: 4.5 tons of silver and gold, gold dust, ivory, indigo and sugar (confirmed by the 1984 excavation)
£20,000+ in coin taken with the Whydah herself, February 1717
pirates.fate: Drowned with 143 others when the Whydah wrecked off Cape Cod in a nor'easter, 26 April 1717; six survivors tried at Boston

track.note: tries wreck-fishing on the 1715 plate-fleet coast with Williams, then turns pirate
elected captain of the Marianne in the Windward Passage when Hornigold's company splits over sparing English ships
cruises the Virgin Islands in company with Olivier La Buse
takes the Sultana near the Saba Bank and makes her flagship
after a three-day chase from the Windward Passage takes the slaver Whydah near Long Island, Bahamas — gold dust, ivory, sugar, indigo
works north taking prizes; storms scatter the squadron off the Virginia Capes, April 1717
the Whydah strikes the bar off Wellfleet, Cape Cod, in a nor'easter, 26 April 1717; Bellamy drowns with 143 men

pirates.name: Edward Thatch (Blackbeard)
pirates.ship_type: frigate (ex-French slaver La Concorde), 40 guns; earlier and later sloops, last the Adventure

flag.description: horned skeleton spearing a heart, hourglass in hand, on black — a later traditional attribution; contemporaries describe only black colours and a death's head

notable_crew.name: Israel Hands
notable_crew.role: sailing master; pardoned, testified

notable_crew.name: William Howard
notable_crew.role: quartermaster

notable_crew.name: Caesar
notable_crew.role: crewman of African birth, taken at Ocracoke (trial records)

lading: sugar, indigo and cotton from Charleston prizes, May 1718
the medicine chest ransomed from Charleston, May 1718
gold dust, sugar and cocoa dispersed when the Queen Anne's Revenge grounded, June 1718
pirates.fate: Killed at Ocracoke by Lt Robert Maynard, 22 November 1718; his head hung from the bowsprit at Hampton Roads

track.note: sails as consort to Hornigold's Ranger along the Cuban coast
with Hornigold takes prizes off the Virginia Capes
cruises off the Delaware Capes with Stede Bonnet's Revenge under his command
takes the slaver La Concorde off St Vincent, 28 November 1717
refits the prize at Bequia as the 40-gun Queen Anne's Revenge
takes the Margaret off Crab Island (Vieques); the squadron grows
at Turneffe in the Bay of Honduras takes the sloop Adventure; four vessels, 60+ guns, ~400 men
blockades Charleston bar for a week, May 1718, ransoming citizens for a medicine chest
runs the Queen Anne's Revenge aground at Beaufort Inlet — likely on purpose — and slips off with the pick of the loot and men, June 1718
takes Governor Eden's pardon at Bath and 'retires' to the North Carolina sounds
pirate revel at Ocracoke with Vane and Rackham, October 1718
killed at Teach's Hole by Lt Maynard's boarders, 22 November 1718 — five pistol balls and twenty sword cuts

pirates.name: Stede Bonnet
pirates.ship_type: 10-gun sloop — bought and fitted out with his own money, uniquely among pirates

flag.description: skull above a single long bone, flanked by a heart and a dagger, on black — traditional attribution

notable_crew.name: David Herriot
notable_crew.role: pilot/sailing master (deposition survives); shot escaping, 1718

notable_crew.name: Ignatius Pell
notable_crew.role: boatswain; turned King's evidence at the trial

lading: sugar, rum and slaves from two Caribbean prizes, 1717
tobacco, flour and sundries from eleven prizes off Virginia and Delaware, summer 1718
pirates.fate: Hanged at White Point, Charles Town, 10 December 1718

track.note: the Barbados planter sails as a pirate — paying his crew wages out of pocket
takes the Anne, Turbet and Endeavour off the Virginia Capes
limps to Nassau wounded after fighting a Spanish warship; Thatch takes over the Revenge, Bonnet aboard as 'guest'
carried in Blackbeard's squadron to the Bay of Honduras
left at Topsail Inlet after Blackbeard strips the company; rescues the marooned men, takes the pardon at Bath, renames his sloop Royal James
back to piracy off Delaware Bay as 'Captain Thomas' — eleven prizes
run to ground in the Cape Fear estuary by Col. William Rhett; taken after a five-hour battle on the sandbars, 27 September 1718

pirates.name: Charles Vane
pirates.ship_type: sloop, later a brigantine of ~12 guns

flag.description: black colours flown (contemporary reports); device unrecorded

notable_crew.name: Jack Rackham
notable_crew.role: quartermaster; deposed him, November 1718

notable_crew.name: Robert Deal
notable_crew.role: mate, then consort captain; hanged at Jamaica

lading: sundry dry goods, rum and coin from Bahamas and Charleston prizes, 1718
pirates.fate: Shipwrecked in the Bay of Honduras 1719; recognized and taken in 1720; hanged at Gallows Point, Port Royal, 29 March 1721

track.note: takes the pardon, relapses within weeks, and plunders the Providence Channel shipping lanes
defies Woodes Rogers with a fireship and runs the harbour mouth, Nassau, 26 July 1718
takes eight ships off the Charleston bar, August 1718
feasts with Blackbeard at Ocracoke, October 1718
declines battle with a French man-of-war, 23 November 1718; branded coward and deposed next day — Rackham elected
hurricane wrecks his sloop on an uninhabited cay off the Bay of Honduras, February 1719; a year a castaway before capture

pirates.name: John 'Calico Jack' Rackham
pirates.ship_type: small Bahamas sloop (stolen from Nassau, 1720); earlier Vane's brigantine and the Kingston prize

flag.description: skull over crossed cutlasses on black — traditional attribution

notable_crew.name: Anne Bonny
notable_crew.role: crew; fought to the last at the capture (trial record)

notable_crew.name: Mary Read
notable_crew.role: crew; 'pleaded her belly' and died in prison, 1721

notable_crew.name: George Fetherston
notable_crew.role: sailing master; hanged 1720

notable_crew.name: Richard Corner
notable_crew.role: quartermaster; hanged 1720

lading: Madeira wine from the Kingston prize, December 1718
fishing tackle, canoes and coasters' sundries from Jamaican waters (1720 trial evidence)
pirates.fate: Hanged at Port Royal, 18 November 1720; gibbeted on the cay now called Rackham's Cay

track.note: elected captain in the Windward Passage when Vane is deposed for cowardice
takes the Madeira-laden Kingston almost within sight of Port Royal, December 1718
loses the Kingston to Jamaican bounty hunters while the crew sports ashore at the Isle of Pines
takes the pardon at Nassau; meets Anne Bonny
steals the sloop William out of Nassau harbour by night, 22 August 1720, Bonny and Read aboard
raids fishing canoes and coasters along Jamaica's north shore
surprised drunk at anchor off Negril Point by Barnet's privateer — the women fight last, late October 1720

pirates.name: Howell Davis
pirates.ship_type: 32-gun prize ship; earlier the sloop Buck and the Saint James

flag.description: black colours hoisted at Gambia and Sierra Leone (Johnson); device unrecorded

notable_crew.name: Bartholomew Roberts
notable_crew.role: forced third mate of the Princess, June 1719 — his successor

notable_crew.name: Walter Kennedy
notable_crew.role: crewman, later renegade captain

notable_crew.name: Thomas Anstis
notable_crew.role: Buck mutineer, later captain

lading: ~£2,000 in gold bar from Fort James, Gambia, taken by masquerade, February 1719
gold dust, slaves and trade goods from Guinea coast prizes, 1719
pirates.fate: Shot in a Portuguese ambush ashore at Príncipe, 19 June 1719

track.note: leads the mutiny aboard the trading sloop Buck off Martinique — elected captain on 'a counterfeit commission from his own brave fancy'
winters at Coxon's Hole on the east end of Cuba
takes Fort James' treasure on the Gambia by posing as a Royal African Company merchant, February 1719
joins Cocklyn and La Buse at Sierra Leone; the three 'gentlemen of fortune' cruise and quarrel together
takes the Princess at Anomabu — third mate Bartholomew Roberts among the forced men, June 1719
killed in a Portuguese ambush ashore at Príncipe, 19 June 1719

pirates.name: Bartholomew Roberts ('Black Bart')
pirates.ship_type: 40-gun ship (ex-RAC frigate Onslow) — the last of several prizes renamed Royal Fortune

flag.description: two documented flags: Roberts and a skeleton holding an hourglass between them; and Roberts standing on two skulls lettered ABH and AMH — 'A Barbadian's Head', 'A Martinican's Head'

notable_crew.name: Henry Glasby
notable_crew.role: forced sailing master; acquitted at Cape Coast

notable_crew.name: James Skyrme
notable_crew.role: captain of the consort Ranger; lost a leg, hanged 1722

notable_crew.name: Walter Kennedy
notable_crew.role: absconded with the Rover and the Bahia loot, 1719; hanged 1721

notable_crew.name: Thomas Anstis
notable_crew.role: deserted with the Good Fortune, April 1721

lading: the Sagrada Família's 40,000 moidores and a diamond-set cross meant for the King of Portugal, Bahia, 1719
sugar, tobacco and hides from the Lisbon-fleet prize and Brazil trade
the Royal Africa Company frigate Onslow and a string of RAC and slaver prizes, 1721-22, with gold dust and coin
pirates.fate: Killed by HMS Swallow's grapeshot off Cape Lopez, 10 February 1722, and buried at sea in his finery; 52 of his men hanged at Cape Coast Castle

track.note: elected captain six weeks after being forced; avenges Davis with a night raid on Príncipe
cuts the Sagrada Família out of the 42-sail Lisbon fleet off Bahia — 40,000 moidores and the king's diamond cross
at the Devil's Islands Kennedy absconds with the Rover and most of the loot
rebuilds his company in the Windward Islands with the little Good Fortune
storms Trepassey harbour, Newfoundland, black flags flying — 22 ships taken, June 1720
takes nine or ten French bankers on the Grand Banks; one becomes the new Royal Fortune
burns shipping in Basseterre road, St Kitts, September 1720
careens at Carriacou, October 1720
throttles Martinique's trade — the island's head joins Barbados' on his flag
crosses for Guinea on the trades, April-May 1721
takes two French ships off the Senegal River, June 1721
refits at Sierra Leone among the old pirate-friendly traders, August 1721; takes the RAC frigate Onslow soon after — the last Royal Fortune
takes eleven slavers in Ouidah road in a day, January 1722; burns the Porcupine with the enslaved aboard
killed at the rail by HMS Swallow's first broadside off Cape Lopez, 10 February 1722; 268 prisoners tried at Cape Coast Castle

pirates.name: Edward England
pirates.ship_type: 34-gun ship; given to Captain Macrae after the Cassandra fight; earlier the Pearl/Royal James

flag.description: black with white skull over crossed bones — the archetypal Jolly Roger (Johnson 1724)

notable_crew.name: John Taylor
notable_crew.role: consort captain of the Victory; led the faction that deposed him

lading: the Cassandra and her cargo, valued ~£75,000, taken at Johanna, August 1720
gold dust, slaves and trade goods from a dozen Guinea-coast prizes, 1719
pirates.fate: Deposed and marooned at Mauritius for sparing Captain Macrae; reached Madagascar on a makeshift boat and died there a beggar, c.1721

track.note: refuses the pardon as Rogers approaches and sails out of Nassau for Africa
takes the Cadogan off Sierra Leone; her captain Skinner murdered by old shipmates
sweeps the Gold Coast — a dozen prizes taken, ransomed or burned (Johnson's list)
careens at Île Sainte-Marie with the Fancy and Taylor's Victory
the Cassandra fight at Johanna, August 1720: Macrae resists for hours, ~90 dead; England takes the ship but spares the captain
deposed for that mercy and marooned on Mauritius by Taylor's faction

pirates.name: John Taylor
pirates.ship_type: ex-East India Company ship Cassandra, ~40 guns

flag.description: black colours reported; device unrecorded

lading: the Nossa Senhora do Cabo's diamonds and treasure, April 1721 — with the gold, silks and church plate, on the order of £800,000: the richest pirate prize of the age
division at Sainte-Marie: 42 small diamonds a man, or one great one
pirates.fate: Surrendered the Cassandra to Spanish authorities at Portobelo, 1723, for a pardon; reportedly ended an officer of the guarda costas

track.note: leads the boarding fight against the Cassandra at Johanna and takes her as flagship
cruises the Malabar coast with La Buse's Victory, dodging EIC squadrons
takes the dismasted Nossa Senhora do Cabo in the Bourbon roadstead with the Goa viceroy aboard, April 1721
the great division at Île Sainte-Marie — diamonds measured out by the handful
winters and refits at Delagoa Bay, December 1721
crosses to the Caribbean and surrenders the Cassandra at Portobelo for a Spanish pardon

pirates.name: Olivier Levasseur ('La Buse')
pirates.ship_type: ship of ~40 guns; earlier the sloop Postillion and others

flag.description: no flag reliably recorded; later legend gives him a death's-head

lading: share of the Nossa Senhora do Cabo's diamonds, gold bars and silks, 1721
by legend, the 'Fiery Cross of Goa' — the unrecovered treasure of his cryptogram (legend, not documented)
pirates.fate: Hanged at Saint-Paul, Bourbon, 7 July 1730 — by legend flinging an encrypted note to the crowd: 'find my treasure who can!'

track.note: consorts with Bellamy and Williams in the Virgin Islands
at Sierra Leone with Cocklyn and Howell Davis; the three captains carouse aboard the Bird Galley prize
wrecks his ship on Mayotte; rebuilds and rejoins the Indian Ocean cruise
with Taylor takes the Nossa Senhora do Cabo at Bourbon — the richest prize of the age, April 1721
at the great division at Sainte-Marie
lives on at Antongil Bay as trader and pilot; balks at amnesty terms that except his plunder
taken off Madagascar, carried to Bourbon; hanged at Saint-Paul, 7 July 1730

pirates.name: Christopher Condent
pirates.ship_type: ex-Dutch ship (the 'Fiery Dragon'), ~40 guns

flag.description: black flag with three skulls-and-crossbones in a row — traditional attribution

lading: an Arab/Surat ship taken off Bombay, October 1720, with coin and goods worth ~£150,000 — ~£2,000-£3,000 a man at the division
pirates.fate: Pardoned at Bourbon 1721; married the governor's sister-in-law and died a prosperous Saint-Malo shipowner

track.note: quits New Providence as Woodes Rogers arrives, bound out across the Atlantic
plunders the salt fleet at Maio and a Dutch war-sloop in the Cape Verdes
harries Portuguese shipping along the Brazil coast
rounds the Cape for the Indian Ocean
careens at Île Sainte-Marie, finding the old base nearly derelict
takes the great Surat ship off Bombay, October 1720 — ~£150,000, and no prisoners abused, by his order
negotiates a pardon at Bourbon; burns the Flying Dragon and settles ashore

sources: de Bry, Fiery Dragon excavation reports

pirates.name: George Lowther
pirates.ship_type: ex-Royal African Company ship Gambia Castle, 16 guns; later the sloop Ranger

flag.description: no flag reliably recorded

notable_crew.name: Edward Low
notable_crew.role: lieutenant from January 1722; split away with 44 men that May

notable_crew.name: John Massey
notable_crew.role: soldier co-mutineer; deserted, hanged 1723

lading: the Gambia Castle's Royal African Company trade cargo, 1721
provisions, rigging and slaver stores from Caribbean and Banks prizes
pirates.fate: Found dead ashore on Blanquilla, October 1723, a discharged pistol beside him — presumed suicide

track.note: second mate Lowther and Capt. Massey's soldiers seize the RAC's Gambia Castle — renamed Happy Delivery, June 1721
takes prizes off the south coast of Hispaniola
at Grand Cayman ships a hard case named Edward Low as lieutenant, January 1722
careening camp in the Gulf of Amatique stormed by local attack; the Happy Delivery lost
Low splits away with 44 men in a prize brigantine, 28 May 1722 (position approximate)
shattered in a fight with the armed merchantman Amy off South Carolina; winters in a North Carolina inlet
rebuilds strength among the Newfoundland fishing fleet
surprised careening at Blanquilla by the sloop Eagle; escapes ashore and is found dead, October 1723

pirates.name: Edward Low
pirates.ship_type: schooner Fancy (ex-Mary of Marblehead), 10 guns; later the Rose Pink and Merry Christmas

flag.description: black flag with a red skeleton (contemporary newspaper descriptions, 1723)

notable_crew.name: Charles Harris
notable_crew.role: consort captain of the Ranger; hanged at Newport with 25 men, July 1723

notable_crew.name: Francis Spriggs
notable_crew.role: crewman, later captain in his own right

lading: fish, provisions and thirteen vessels' gear from Port Roseway, June 1722
wine and sundries from Azores and Canaries prizes, 1722-23
logwood from the Bay of Honduras
pirates.fate: Fate unrecorded: reported variously as marooned by his own crew and as hanged by the French at Martinique, c.1724

track.note: turns pirate in the Bay of Honduras after a quarrel over logwood pay; falls in with Lowther soon after
takes thirteen fishing vessels at Port Roseway, Nova Scotia; keeps the schooner Mary as the Fancy, June 1722
plunders shipping in the road of São Miguel, Azores; the cruelties that made his name begin
works down through the Canaries and Cape Verdes; a failed dash for Brazil
massacres the crew of a Spanish sloop that had taken English logwood-men, Bay of Honduras, March 1723
mauled by HMS Greyhound off Long Island, 10 June 1723; consort Ranger taken — 26 hanged at Newport — Low flees and turns crueller
tortures and murders whaling skipper Nathan Skiff off Nantucket
back to the Azores in the Merry Christmas, flying his red-skeleton black flag
last credible reports in the eastern Caribbean; thereafter rumour only (position conjectural)
